cards.definition: What happens to the bean inside a coffee fruit, on gentle squeeze, when the fruit is properly ripe and ready to pick.
cards.analogy: Like a ripe grape releasing its seed with almost no resistance, versus an unripe one that won't let go.
cards.why_it_matters: A fast, hands-on way to sort a picker's basket for ripeness without needing instruments.
cards.cloze: A ripe coffee fruit is identified when, on gentle squeeze, the bean ___.

cards.definition: The specific cup and bean defect that results from pulping fruit that was picked past its ripeness window.
cards.analogy: Fruit left too long on the vine before processing, the way over-ripe produce turns fermented and off rather than merely soft.
cards.why_it_matters: A concrete reason picking rounds need to be timed tightly, not just 'whenever there's time.'
cards.cloze: Over-ripe coffee fruits, if pulped for parchment, cause ___.
mcq_options: foxy coffee with brown beans and fruity cup

cards.definition: The specific mould-linked contaminant that picking mats help suppress by keeping fruit off bare, contaminated ground during harvest.
cards.analogy: A groundsheet doing double duty — catching fallen fruit and keeping it off the exact surface mould loves.
cards.why_it_matters: A food-safety detail as much as a quality one — this contaminant is regulated in export markets.
cards.cloze: Use of picking mats during harvest reduces mould formation and helps prevent the production of ___ in coffee beans.

cards.definition: The rule on whether bags that previously held agrochemicals are an acceptable container for harvested coffee fruit.
cards.analogy: The same logic as never reusing a pesticide drum to store drinking water — residue risk doesn't wash out reliably.
cards.why_it_matters: A simple, zero-cost habit to check for on any farm visit — the bags in use tell you a lot about food-safety awareness.
cards.cloze: Bags previously used for storing chemicals, fertilizers, pesticides or fungicides should ___ be used for harvesting coffee fruit.

cards.definition: The general quality relationship between parchment-processed (washed) coffee and cherry-processed (natural/dry) coffee.
cards.analogy: Not a universal ranking so much as a documented tendency — washed processing removes more variables that can go wrong.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful context when a farmer or trader frames 'parchment vs cherry' as purely a price distinction rather than a quality one.
cards.cloze: The quality of parchment coffee is ___ to that of cherry coffee.
